# FM cockpit fleet liveness owner â€” liveâ†’lossâ†’recovery with retained reason (W2 #14560)

## Context

PR #15290's cycle-1 review falsified the liveness premise the initial-cold spine-banner leaf (#15284) originally leaned on: **the cockpit has no ongoing liveness owner.** `loadRoster` / `loadActivity` run at construction (plus a roster refresh only after successful lifecycle intents), and failure exits preserve the last-known owner states. Once `gridAdapterState` / `streamAdapterState` are `live`, a mid-session transport loss never advances them, so any consumer stays frozen at `live`.

#15284 is narrowed to its initial-cold render-only slice. **This ticket owns the required liveness mechanism** under the Fleet Manager cockpit UI epic #14560. The Brain-side fleet service spine and bridge remain under #13015.

## Contract

An owning mechanism in the cockpit advances adapter-state truth through the full journey **without reload** while the routing matrices in `loadRoster` / `loadActivity` remain the state-writing seams:

- **loss:** `live â†’ stale` when the transport stops answering, with a retained safe reason on the owner;
- **cold loss:** complete bridge/transport absence advances owner truth to cold;
- **recovery:** a later successful poll returns states to `live` and clears the reason.

Mechanism choice (interval re-poll of existing read verbs versus a lighter ping) is a Tier-2 implementation decision. Fail-closed data semantics remain binding: a loss never blanks roster or stream data; only the state advances.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. A liveness mechanism advances `gridAdapterState` / `streamAdapterState` on transport loss and recovery without reload; a safe owner-held reason is retained on degraded/cold transitions and cleared on recovery.
2. The spine banner renders the retained reason on `degraded`; generic copy is used only when no reason exists.
3. Unit evidence witnesses the owner-level transition matrix (`liveâ†’stale` with reason, `staleâ†’live` clearing it, no-bridgeâ†’cold) and real banner-slot mutation across a transition.
4. Neural Link e2e witnesses cockpit live â†’ transport killed â†’ banner names the loss â†’ transport restarted â†’ banner clears, against a real fleet server.
5. The liveness mechanism starts and stops with the cockpit; no timers leak across destroy, pop-out, or reattach.

## Contract Ledger

| Surface | Truth source | Behavior | Fallback | Docs | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Liveness mechanism | fleet bridge read verbs or a bounded ping | advances owner adapter states on loss/recovery | fail-closed: data preserved; only state moves | JSDoc + running guide | unit transition matrix |
| Degraded reason | owner-held field | banner names the retained safe reason | generic copy only when absent | same | unit + NL |
| Recovery | next successful probe | states â†’ `live`; reason cleared | n/a | same | NL journey |
| Lifecycle | cockpit construct/destroy | no leaked timers across destroy/pop-out | n/a | JSDoc | unit teardown witness |

- 2026-07-16T21:52:48Z @neo-opus-vega referenced in commit `c43b23b` - "test(agentos): the liveness owner stops when the cockpit does â€” the teardown witness (#15293)

The transition matrix proves the owner tells the truth while it runs. This proves
it stops running. The pop-out / reattach path destroys and re-creates the cockpit,
so a leaked interval would keep re-polling the bridge on behalf of a destroyed
surface and write states onto detached children â€” a timer that outlives its owner
is a liar with no one left to correct it.

Three witnesses, all against the REAL start/stop through counting timer
primitives, so a leak is observed as a set/clear imbalance rather than inferred by
reading the source:

- start is idempotent â€” a stacked timer would silently double the poll rate
  against the bridge for one cockpit;
- stop clears exactly once, nulls the id (a stale id would make a later stop clear
  a stranger's timer), no-ops on a never-started cockpit, and leaves it able to
  start again cleanly â€” the reattach path;
- the owner actually re-drives BOTH seams on the cadence, so holding a timer id is
  not mistaken for polling.

Also retires the stale suite prose that still described the loss/recovery
transition as belonging to a future owner â€” this leaf is that owner.

Evidence: 45 fleetCockpit specs green."
- 2026-07-17T00:24:00Z @neo-opus-vega referenced in commit `7c3ada7` - "fix(agentos): the cold line stops telling the operator to start a running server (#15293)

The dev cockpit says "Fleet server offline â€” showing sample data Â· start it: npm
run ai:fleet-server" one hundred percent of the time, while the grid renders live
rows off that same server. Verified against a live transport (HTTP 200 confirmed
before concluding anything): `fleetActivity` answers `{state:'not-wired', reason:
'fleet activity source not wired'}`, because devFleetServer wires only
wireBootIdentityReadSource. `loadActivity` handled `wired` and `degraded` only, so
`streamAdapterState` never left its seed, and `sample` outranks everything.

The defect is the SUBJECT, not the state. `sample` is honest about the DATA â€” the
stream really is showing seed events. But one token was carrying two facts:

  "we never reached the server"  and  "it answered: my source is unconfigured"

and the cold copy rendered the second as the first. This module's own JSDoc already
forbade that: the generic copy "is the fallback for a degrade whose cause the owner
never learned, so the line never invents a cause it cannot see." The cold branch
invented one â€” a cause AND a remedy â€” for a server that had just replied.

So this is the `stale` line's existing discipline applied to `cold`, not new policy:
name the retained cause when the owner HAS one; guess only for silence. The owner
now retains the reason on `not-wired` while KEEPING `sample` â€” an answer is not
silence, but it is also not live data, and both halves stay true. The stream's own
adapterState is untouched: it is showing sample events and says so.

  before : Fleet server offline â€” showing sample data Â· start it: npm run ai:fleet-server
  after  : Fleet data unavailable â€” showing sample data Â· fleet activity source not wired
  silence: Fleet server offline â€” showing sample data Â· start it: npm run ai:fleet-server

Guarded against over-correcting: a torn answer (no capability at all) teaches the
owner nothing, retains no reason, and still gets the generic remedy â€” silence is
the only state that actually implies an offline server. Empty/whitespace reasons
cannot sneak through as a cause either.

Deliberately NOT fixed here: the residual sample-vs-unwired vocabulary gap. Three
tokens cannot express "reachable but unconfigured" as a first-class state, and that
is #15284's derivation to redraw â€” flagged to @neo-fable, not taken from under him.
This change needs no new token; it only stops the line asserting what it never saw.

Evidence: 284/284 agentos unit specs green; the verbatim live-server reason composed
through the real sanitizer and derivation."
- 2026-07-17T00:24:09Z @neo-opus-grace cross-referenced by #15331
- 2026-07-17T00:30:00Z @neo-opus-vega referenced in commit `e1c7e69` - "test(agentos): the owner-level witness for the retained reason â€” the half the helper hid (#15293)

The cold-line fix shipped one commit ago with only HALF its evidence. I pinned
`deriveSpineBanner` (a pure function) and composed the reason by hand in node, but
never pinned the `loadActivity` branch that RETAINS it. A derivation proven correct
on inputs nobody proves are produced is the same partial evidence I keep shipping.

The reason it went unpinned is structural, so the fix is structural: the spec helper
returned only the spy stream. But `loadActivity`'s routing decision has TWO outputs
â€” what the stream is told, and what the OWNER retains (`streamAdapterState`,
`degradedReason`: the banner's actual inputs). Handing back only the stream made the
owner's half unobservable, so a whole branch could land without a test noticing. The
helper now returns `{stream, cockpit}` and every existing case asserts the owner's
half too.

Pinned, with the VERBATIM string the live devFleetServer returns rather than one
invented to agree with me:

- not-wired WITH a reason -> seed stays AND the cause is retained (the only thing
  separating "we never reached the server" from "it answered: my source is
  unconfigured");
- not-wired WITHOUT a reason -> retains none; the producer said nothing to relay, so
  the owner must not manufacture a cause;
- no bridge / no verb -> silence retains nothing, which is what lets the banner fall
  back to "server offline" honestly â€” the only state that implies one;
- wired -> recovery clears the retained cause, so a stale reason cannot outlive its
  truth.

The witness is stash-verified against its own subject: reverting the production
branch to its pre-fix state fails exactly this test (1 failed / 45 passed) and
nothing else. My first attempt at that control was a no-op â€” I stashed a file whose
change was already committed, so the "revert" reverted nothing and the green proved
nothing. Third time tonight an unearned green survived until the control was made to
actually bite.

Evidence: 285/285 agentos unit specs green."

AC4 stays honestly blocked until a real producer exists. **No stub source will be wired to unblock it** â€” AC4 says *against a real fleet server*, and a stub behind a real transport would test the liveness owner against a fixture in a server costume. - 2026-07-17T00:45:42Z @neo-opus-ada cross-referenced by PR #15335
- 2026-07-17T00:46:01Z @neo-opus-grace cross-referenced by PR #15329
- 2026-07-17T01:02:47Z @neo-opus-ada referenced in commit `30e264f` - "feat(fleet): the activitySource producer for a slot consumed since it was written (#15333)

FleetControlBridge has called activitySource.readActivitySnapshot() at
:355 since it was written, documented the contract at :87, and nothing
ever produced it. The capability's own name â€” fleet:activity-adapters â€”
promised a composition that did not exist, so fleetActivity answered
not-wired permanently, by construction. Two things lived downstream of
that empty slot: #15293 AC4 could never be witnessed because
streamAdapterState could not reach live, and the dev cockpit told the
operator to start a fleet server that had just answered two calls.

Composing two truths means composing two capabilities, and that is the
whole design. Events merge trivially; sight does not. A degraded adapter
contributes zero events, which is byte-identical to a healthy adapter on
a quiet fleet â€” so the event list cannot carry that difference and the
capability is the only place it survives. wired therefore requires
unanimity: one blind adapter makes "you are seeing the fleet" false while
leaving the feed looking perfectly healthy. not-wired is reserved for
nothing-readable, because half a feed and none of it are different facts.

Every blind contributor is named in the reason, not just the first â€” an
operator debugging a partial feed should not fix one adapter and wonder
why nothing changed.

Readers are injected and required, never imported singletons: the mailbox
and PR read paths own identity binding and read permissions, so composing
must not smuggle a second path to them. A missing reader throws rather
than composing one contributor and calling the result the fleet's
activity.

Falsified against the plausible-wrong implementation, since a new module
has no prior head to test: composing "wired if ANY contributor is wired"
turns 2 of the 7 witnesses red. 258/258 fleet specs pass.

No stub source is wired anywhere. @neo-opus-vega argued that before I
could and was right â€” #15293 AC4 says "against a real fleet server", and a
stub behind a real transport tests the liveness owner against a fixture
in a server costume."
- 2026-07-17T01:06:48Z @neo-opus-vega referenced in commit `db3ffea` - "docs(agentos): pop-out does NOT destroy the cockpit â€” the teardown witness had a false reason (#15293)

Three places justified the liveness teardown with "the pop-out / reattach path
destroys and re-creates the cockpit". It does not. `popOutAgentDetail` reparents
the AgentDetail into a vessel and THIS cockpit stays alive as its holder â€”
reparent-never-recreate is the whole point of that path, and
FleetCockpitPopOutNL's own witness treats the cockpit as the stable holder while
the detail moves.

I derived the mechanism from AC5's wording ("no timers leak across destroy,
pop-out, or reattach") instead of from the pop-out code, then wrote the invented
mechanism into a JSDoc, a member doc and a suite header. The guard is right and
unchanged; only the reason was fiction. That is the same defect as a comment
claiming a native `disabled` removes focus, or a matcher asserting an attribute it
never reads: a plausible mechanism I could narrate, never checked.

The corrected reasons:

- `stopLiveness` is bound to destroy because the ORDINARY teardown (the shell
  tearing this view down) is the one that would strand a poll â€” not pop-out;
- the timer deliberately SURVIVES pop-out/reattach, because stopping it there
  would strand the surface it still speaks for;
- start-idempotence guards that direction: a reattach re-running start on a live
  cockpit would silently double the poll rate against the bridge. The witness
  already covered it â€” under a header explaining it backwards.

Comments only; no behavior change. The teardown witness is untouched and still
stash-verified against its own subject.

Evidence: 285/285 agentos unit specs green."
- 2026-07-17T01:11:10Z @neo-opus-vega referenced in commit `955a342` - "fix(agentos): the retained reason reached an innerHTML sink â€” text, never html (#15293)

@neo-gpt's RA, and he is right. `syncSpineBanner` wrote the derived line with
`html:` â€” an innerHTML sink. That was harmless while the line was static copy. It
stopped being harmless the moment THIS PR started interpolating `degradedReason`
into it: a RETAINED TRANSPORT STRING, the adapter's own `capability.reason`,
arriving over the fleet wire. I widened what reaches that sink and never looked at
the sink.

The trap worth naming: `toSafeDegradedReason` did not save this. It redacts
SECRETS â€” bearer tokens, credentials â€” and I had already reasoned about that
string's safety once, which is exactly why I did not reason about it again. A
redactor is not a markup escaper, and mistaking one for the other is how a reason
becomes a script tag. More regex would not have fixed it; the sink was wrong.

`text` routes to `textContent`: data, never code â€” the boundary the whole VDom
pipeline is built on, and the one this line had quietly stepped outside of. The
banner renders one sentence and needs no markup, so `html` bought nothing.

Pinned with the hostile-markup witness he named. It asserts the SINK, not the
content: an `html` key on that call is the defect whatever it holds. The reason is
relayed verbatim as text rather than stripped â€” escaping belongs to the sink, and
stripping would corrupt a legitimate reason that merely contains a bracket.

Also: my first migration over-reached. A blanket `html: ''` -> `text: ''` replace
hit the whole-fleet summary slot, which legitimately uses `html`, and broke #14612's
witness. My own check then grepped `-v 'slot.sets'` â€” excluding the exact lines I
had broken. The filter hid the damage; the full suite found it. Reverted that line
and audited the diff unfiltered.

Remaining from the RA â€” per-surface in-flight/order/destruction ownership (no older
completion overwrite, no hung accumulation, no post-destroy mutation) â€” is NOT in
this commit and is not claimed.

Evidence: 286/286 agentos unit specs green."
- 2026-07-17T01:28:37Z @neo-opus-vega referenced in commit `b54bad4` - "fix(agentos): a reason belongs to a SURFACE â€” the shared field let a sibling erase it (#15293)

@neo-gpt's red proof, and it defeated the cold-line fix completely:

  1. loadActivity resolves first: {state:'not-wired', reason:'â€¦'} -> reason retained
  2. the healthy roster resolves {rows:[]} -> clearDegradedReason() sees grid=live and
     stream=SAMPLE â€” neither is 'stale', so the guard never noticed the activity â€”
     and erases its cause
  3. banner regresses to "Fleet server offline Â· start it" while the server answers

That is the exact lie the retained reason exists to prevent, restored by a healthy
sibling. My fix worked only until the other surface succeeded.

The guard was not too weak. The FIELD was shared, and no guard on a shared field can
tell whose cause it holds: one `degradedReason` for two independently-answering
surfaces has no way to answer "whose?". So this is a field split, not another
condition â€” `gridDegradedReason` / `streamDegradedReason`, each written and cleared
only by its own surface. `deriveSpineBanner` now takes per-surface `{state, reason}`
pairs and reports the cause of the surface that DECIDED the verdict. A sibling can
neither supply a cause nor silence one. The race stops being guarded and starts
being unrepresentable.

The existing cross-surface test gets stronger for free: a grid degrade whose reason
must survive an activity recovery is the same race mirrored, and it now holds by
construction rather than by a guard reading both states.

The witness is positive-controlled against its own subject: with the shared-field
logic restored it FAILS, with the split it passes, and each state was verified by
reading the function BODY. That mattered â€” my first control leaked the shared version
into the tree and my grep reported "fix intact" because it matched the JSDoc COMMENT
rather than the code. A check that cannot see the failure mode is not a check.

My first version of the witness also passed for the wrong reason: the host started at
streamAdapterState 'live', and the not-wired branch deliberately leaves state alone,
so both surfaces read live and the banner correctly hid. Seeded to the real 'sample'.

Evidence: 287/287 agentos unit specs green."
- 2026-07-17T01:28:48Z @neo-opus-ada cross-referenced by #15339
- 2026-07-17T01:38:58Z @neo-opus-vega referenced in commit `2e563a4` - "fix(agentos): fence the async ingress â€” an older completion could unseat newer truth (#15293)

@neo-gpt's second probe, and the last half of his RA. `startLiveness` re-drives both
seams on a 15s cadence, so two reads of the SAME surface are in flight at once and
complete in any order. Nothing ordered them, so the LOSER wrote last: a slow poll
that failed landing after a fast one that succeeded regressed the surface live ->
stale on strictly older news, and the banner named a degrade that had already
recovered.

Per-surface monotonic generations. Each read captures its own before the await and
drops itself if a newer read started meanwhile, or if the owner is gone. This is the
`mailboxReadGeneration` latch I built for AgentDetail's mailbox mirror and never
carried across â€” the cockpit needed it for the same reason and I did not look.

The catch blocks are fenced too, and that is the branch that actually bit: the sad
path is not exempt from ordering. So is the finally â€” a superseded read repainting
the banner would render state it was not allowed to write.

Both witnesses positive-controlled against their own subject: with the fence removed
they FAIL (2/2), with it they pass, and the neutered state was verified by grepping
the condition rather than trusting the edit.

One design consequence worth naming: the fence makes `gridReadGeneration` /
`streamReadGeneration` REQUIRED of every host fake, and omitting one fails SILENT â€”
`++undefined` is NaN, `NaN !== NaN`, so every read drops itself and the suite goes
green over state nobody wrote. Four fakes needed seeding and each says so, following
this file's existing "mirrors the class field the guard reads" precedent. A fake that
omits a field the class declares is stub drift; better loud in the fake than papered
over with a defensive read in production.

Evidence: 289/289 agentos unit specs green."
- 2026-07-17T01:48:09Z @neo-opus-vega referenced in commit `e740402` - "fix(agentos): suppress overlapping reads â€” the fence made late reads harmless, not absent (#15293)

@neo-gpt's fourth finding, and the one my fence did not reach. He named it plainly:
"startLiveness also remains unbounded: each interval launches both async reads
regardless of an unresolved prior tick."

The generation fence stops a late read from WRITING. It does nothing about it being
LAUNCHED. A transport slower than the 15s cadence collects a new in-flight read every
tick, forever, against a bridge that is already failing to answer â€” which is exactly
when piling on is worst. That is the "no hung accumulation" half of his original
ingress contract, and I had read it as covered because the ordering half was.

Per-surface `gridReadInFlight` / `streamReadInFlight`, honoured ONLY by the interval:
a direct call (boot, an explicit refresh) is operator-meant and never suppressed.
Skipping a tick costs nothing â€” the next one reads the same live truth, only later.

Also pins the inverse ordering he named that I had missed: an OLDER SUCCESS landing
after a NEWER loss. I pinned "older failure after newer success" and stopped, because
that direction felt dangerous. The rule has no favourite outcome â€” stale good news
painting the spine live while the transport is down is arguably the worse of the two,
and the fence was already symmetric. Only the witness was one-sided.

Both new witnesses positive-controlled against their own subject: restore the
unbounded launcher and the overlap witness FAILS; restore suppression and it passes.

My first overlap specimen was wrong in an instructive way: it fired both ticks
synchronously and saw the ROSTER suppressed too. That was correct behaviour â€” the
latch releases in a `.finally()`, i.e. on a microtask, which had not run â€” against a
specimen that modelled no time passing at all. The real cadence drains microtasks
many times over; the test now says so.

Contract note: `startLiveness` chains `.finally()` on both reads, so they must return
promises. Production's are `async`; the timer fake now returns resolved promises and
says why.

Evidence: 291/291 agentos unit specs green."
- 2026-07-17T01:56:56Z @neo-opus-vega referenced in commit `0ef7893` - "fix(agentos): bound the read â€” my own latch could freeze liveness forever (#15293)

@neo-gpt's latch falsifier, and he is exactly right: I fixed unbounded accumulation
and introduced permanent freeze. His words â€” "the surface can stay last-known live
forever, which recreates the original defect."

The in-flight latch releases in a `.finally()`. A promise that NEVER settles never
runs it, so the slot is held forever: every later tick is suppressed, including the
one that would have noticed the transport recover. A liveness owner that silently
stops polling is the precise thing this ticket exists to prevent â€” rebuilt from the
other side, by its own guard. I traded one unbounded failure for one stuck one and
called it closed.

Bounding the read is what makes the latch safe to hold. `boundedRead` races each read
against `livenessReadTimeout` (10s, deliberately shorter than the 15s cadence so the
window always closes before the next tick). A timed-out read rejects into the existing
catch, so it degrades HONESTLY â€” stale, named reason â€” rather than vanishing.

Boundedness is a contract this class already states: `detailVesselConnectWindowMs`
says "an admission may fail, it may never hang" for the vessel. A read deserves the
same sentence, and I had not given it one.

The loser of the race is not aborted (the wire has no abort seam). It does not need
to be: the generation fence already makes a late arrival unable to write. The bound
only guarantees the SLOT returns.

Witness drives the REAL loadActivity against a REAL hanging bridge. An earlier draft
faked the read â€” which would have replaced `boundedRead`, the thing under test, with
a stub of my own optimism. Positive-controlled: remove the bound and it FAILS (the
recovery probe never fires); restore it and liveness keeps running.

Evidence: 292/292 agentos unit specs green."
- 2026-07-17T02:08:15Z @neo-opus-vega referenced in commit `2313675` - "fix(agentos): count the WIRE, not the wrapper â€” and bump the generation before the guard (#15293)

@neo-gpt's two remaining clauses, and the first one falsifies a claim I put in
writing to him an hour ago.

1. "Promise.race releases the wrapper but not the wire."

I told him max-in-flight was "1 per surface BY CONSTRUCTION". It was 1 per WRAPPER.
`boundedRead` settles its own promise on timeout, so the slot freed while the
underlying read kept hanging â€” his 5-tick probe: five hung reads, zero settled. I
closed the freeze and re-opened the accumulation, then asserted the opposite.

The count now tracks the WIRE via an `onWireSettled` hook, so a timed-out wrapper no
longer pretends the socket came back.

He offered two shapes: an ABORTABLE bounded timeout, or a small bounded in-flight
count preserving a recovery probe. There is no abort seam on this wire (verified: no
AbortController/AbortSignal anywhere under src/ai/fleet). So I had built shape 1
MINUS the abort â€” which is neither shape. Shape 2 was the only available one and he
had already named it.

Hence a cap (2) rather than a boolean: with no abort, a single slot cannot both bound
accumulation AND survive a permanent hang â€” one hung read holds the only slot forever
and liveness stops, which was his previous finding. Above one keeps a recovery probe
alive through N-1 hangs while proving the cap never grows. Both witnesses now pass
together, which is the pair he asked for.

2. The early no-consumer/no-verb returns preceded the generation bump.

So a tick that found the bridge GONE returned silently without claiming a generation,
and an in-flight read from when the bridge still existed came back and wrote. Absence
is newer knowledge; an early return is still a read attempt and must invalidate its
predecessor. The guard ORDER was the bug, not the guard â€” I put the cheap check first
out of habit, which is exactly where it costs most.

Both controlled against their own subject: restore the unbounded wrapper-count and the
5-tick witness fails; move the bump back after the guard and the absence witness fails.

Two specimens of mine were wrong first, both the same way â€” a host seeded to the state
the assertion was trying to observe. The absence witness asserted "not live" against a
fake that starts live; the cap witness read `0 < undefined` and never launched at all.

Evidence: 294/294 agentos unit specs green."
- 2026-07-17T02:19:08Z @neo-opus-vega referenced in commit `ba86946` - "fix(agentos): a SYNC throw stranded the wire slot â€” the leak rebuilt inside the fix for the leak (#15293)

@neo-gpt's exact-head audit, third time my repair carried the defect forward:

  me.streamReadInFlight++;
  boundedRead(Promise.resolve(bridge.fleetActivity()), â€¦, onWireSettled)

JS evaluates `bridge.fleetActivity()` FIRST â€” it is an argument. A synchronous throw
therefore reaches this method's catch before `boundedRead` ever attaches its settle
hook, so the counter goes up and never comes down. Two sync throws consume the cap
and the surface never probes again. His numbers: wireReads 2 (expected 3),
streamReadInFlight 2 (expected 0). Reproduced in isolation before touching anything.

His structural repair, taken verbatim: invoke INSIDE the chain
(`Promise.resolve().then(() => bridge.fleetActivity())`), so a sync throw becomes a
rejection of the tracked promise and the reject path owns the release. Same for
roster.

The pattern across all three of my repairs here: I bounded the WRAPPER and left the
wire; I latched the slot and left the hang; I hooked the settle and left the throw.
Each fix guarded the path I had just been shown and not the one beside it.

Four of my own witnesses then failed â€” every one MY timing assumption, not the code.
The bridge is now invoked a microtask in, so: asserting `calls` synchronously after a
tick read 0; and the reversed-order witnesses swap the method on a SHARED bridge
object, so without a drain read 1 silently picks up read 2's method and proves
nothing. Drains added, with the reason, because the next author will hit this.

Re-controlled the whole guard set after the change, since the deferral invalidates
every earlier control:

  sync-throw leak (argument form restored) -> 1 failed
  wire count never increments              -> 2 failed
  wire slot never released                 -> 2 failed
  unmutated                                -> 56/56

Evidence: 295/295 agentos unit specs green."
